findings.summary: No contradictions found in reviewed plan and phase files.
No uncovered phase output gap found in reviewed plan and phase files.
No ambiguous phase instruction found beyond the write conflict reported separately.
Wave 1 is marked parallel, but PHASE-XX-a and PHASE-XX-b both write src/auth/login.ts.
findings.remediation: Give src/auth/login.ts ownership to one phase and make the other phase read it, or split the two phases into separate waves so JWT issuance and rate-limit wiring are sequenced.
findings.why_real_risk: The pairwise write-scope audit for Wave 1 finds an intersection: PHASE-XX-a writes src/auth/login.ts and PHASE-XX-b also writes src/auth/login.ts. Parallel execution can produce incompatible edits to the same file, with one engineer's patch failing or overwriting the other's login changes.
findings.repro: Dispatch Wave 1 in parallel: engineer A implements JWT issuance in src/auth/login.ts while engineer B wires rate limiting in src/auth/login.ts. When both patches are applied, the second patch touches a file already changed by the first phase, so either git apply conflicts or the later edit loses part of the JWT or middleware integration.
tests_to_add: Add a plan-validation fixture asserting that two phases in the same wave with shared writes to src/auth/login.ts are rejected before dispatch.
Add a disjointness check that reports the phase pair and shared write path for every intra-wave write intersection.

findings.summary: No completeness issue found apart from the parallel write conflict reported separately.
No architectural fit issue found in the reviewed plan and phase files.
No duplication or needless abstraction issue found in the reviewed plan and phase files.
No professionalism issue found in the reviewed plan and phase files.
